content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
July 7,
1994
William C. Katzenstein
Office of the County Counsel
Riverside County
3535 Tenth Street,
Suite 300
Riverside,
CA
92501—3674
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-16l
Dear Mr. Katzenstein:
I
am writing to confirm our telephone conversation on
June 16,
1994, wherein we discussed your request for advice
regarding whether the members of County Service Area Advisory Com
mittee 124
(C.S.A.
124)
are considered public officials under the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
I informed you that based on the facts provided,
members of
C.S.A.
124 are public officials and are subject to the disclosure
and disqualification requirements of the Act.
I suggested that
you speak directly to Ms.
Dixie Howard of our Technical Assistance
Division for assistance with amending the county’s conflict-of—
interest code to include C.S.A.
124 members.
I also sent you Regulations 18703 and 18703.3, regarding the
public generally exceptions and Advice Letter A-93-229.
The facts provided in your letter stated that a county acting
under Government Code Section 25210.1 et seq.
is authorized to
establish county service areas to perform one or more of the
various functions enumerated in Government Code Sections 25210.4
and 25210.4a.
In County Resolution 81-60,
adopted by the
Riverside County Board of Supervisors on November 14,
1978, the
board established an Advisory Committee for C.S.A.
124.
Under the
wording of the resolution,
members selected for the committee must
be either “resident voters or owners of real property within the
boundaries
...“ of the C.S.A.
and are appointed by the board.
Under the provisions of the resolution,
the committee is
“...
to
study problems of general or specific interest to the board and to
make reports and recommendations to the board on matters relating
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Divi—
sion
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—94—l61
Page
2
to road maintenance and improvement
...“ for County Service Area
124 and to
“...
recommend to the board an annual budget for said
Service Area.”
(Section 252l0.4a (15).)
The committee meets a minimum of four times a year and
establishes a budget for County Service Area 124.
This budget is
practically always accepted by the County Administrative Office
(which in practice oversees the operation of all county service
areas in Riverside County)
and results in assessments being placed
on most lands within the service area, with the exception of a few
lands that are exempted by law from assessments.
The assessments
are in an amount sufficient to raise the money specified in the
annual budget.
Thereafter, the committee meets on a periodic basis and votes
to decide which specific roads are to be paved with the money that
has been raised from the assessments.
In taking their actions,
committee members stand to benefit directly or indirectly from the
roads graded or paved and to suffer financial burdens in ac
cordance with the number of parcels of land on which they are pay
ing assessments.
The decision on the budget and any decisions on the roads
that are to be graded or paved are only infrequently revised or
sent back to the committee by the County Administrative Office.
In most cases, the decisions of the advisory committee on both the
budget and the improvements to be made are not forwarded to the
County Board of Supervisors for review or approval,
but are
directly implemented by the County Administrative Office and by
the various county departments charged with responsibilities in
these areas.
Based on the above facts,
the members of C.S.A.
124 should be
designated in the county’s conflict—of—interest code,
as we
discussed.
I trust this answers your question.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
q’i)ll
techer
óunsel,
Legal Division

Ms.
Dixie Howard

sections.question_synthetic: Whether members of County Service Area Advisory Committee 124 are considered public officials under the Political Reform Act and are subject to disclosure and disqualification requirements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Members of C.S.A. 124 are public officials under the Political Reform Act and are subject to disclosure and disqualification requirements. The county should designate C.S.A. 124 members in the county's conflict-of-interest code.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an FPPC response letter confirming prior telephone advice. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections are marked, but the question and conclusion are clearly stated in the body text. Letter discusses a specific advisory committee's status under Political Reform Act based on their budgetary and project decision-making authority.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether members of County Service Area Advisory Committee 124 are considered public officials under the Political Reform Act and are subject to disclosure and disqualification requirements.

CONCLUSION: Members of C.S.A. 124 are public officials under the Political Reform Act and are subject to disclosure and disqualification requirements. The county should designate C.S.A. 124 members in the county's conflict-of-interest code.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission July 7, 1994 William C. Katzenstein Office of the County Counsel Riverside County 3535 Tenth Street, Suite 300 Riverside, CA 92501—3674 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-94-16l Dear Mr. Katzenstein: I am writing to confirm our telephone conversation on June 16, 1994, wherein we discussed your request for advice regarding whether the members of County Service Area Advisory Com mittee 124 (C.S.A. 124) are considered public officials under the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 I informed you that based on the facts provided, members of C.S.A. 124 are public officials and are subject to the disclosure and disqualification requirements of the Act. I suggested that you speak directly to Ms. Dixie Howard of our Technical Assistance Division for assistance with amending the county’s conflict-of— interest code to include C.S.A. 124 members. I also sent you Regulations 18703 and 18703.3, regarding the public generally exceptions and Advice Letter A-93-229. The facts provided in your letter stated that a county acting under Government Code Section 25210.1 et seq. is authorized to establish county service areas to perform one or more of the various functions enumerated in Government Code Sections 25210.4 and 25210.4a. In County Resolution 81-60, adopted by the Riverside County Board of Supervisors on November 14, 1978, the board established an Advisory Committee for C.S.A. 124. Under the wording of the resolution, members selected for the committee must be either “resident voters or owners of real property within the boundaries ...“ of the C.S.A. and are appointed by the board. Under the provisions of the resolution, the committee is “... to study problems of general or specific interest to the board and to make reports and recommendations to the board on matters relating 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Divi— sion 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 File No. A—94—l61 Page 2 to road maintenance and improvement ...“ for County Service Area 124 and to “... recommend to the board an annual budget for said Service Area.” (Section 252l0.4a (15).) The committee meets a minimum of four times a year and establishes a budget for County Service Area 124. This budget is practically always accepted by the County Administrative Office (which in practice oversees the operation of all county service areas in Riverside County) and results in assessments being placed on most lands within the service area, with the exception of a few lands that are exempted by law from assessments. The assessments are in an amount sufficient to raise the money specified in the annual budget. Thereafter, the committee meets on a periodic basis and votes to decide which specific roads are to be paved with the money that has been raised from the assessments.
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter confirming that members of Riverside County Service Area Advisory Committee 124 are public officials subject to the Political Reform Act's disclosure and disqualification requirements, based on their substantial decision-making authority over budget and road improvement funding that directly affects their financial interests.
